data.title: Veeva Systems: Not A Likely Victim Of Total AI Disruption - Buy The Dip (Upgrade)
data.description:
Though the stock market has managed to quickly claw back all of the losses since the Iran conflict began, underneath a flat YTD S&P 500 (SP500) is a deep recession in software stocks, prompted by fears of the "SaaSpocalypse." Previously the hottest corner of the stock market since COVID, software stocks have undergone a deep re-rating in valuation multiples that have erased years of gains.

Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV), a stock I had frequently pointed out as overvalued, has lost ~30% of its value this year, wiping billions of dollars off its market cap. At the same time, however, fundamental sales execution remains healthy. The core question now: is it time to buy the dip, or is Veeva just a falling knife?

I was bearish on Veeva last year when the stock was trading in the ~$300s, and I recently upgraded the stock to a neutral stance in January, when the stock had made its first descent to the ~$220s. To me, the most significant asset an investor can have during volatile markets is an ability to change our minds. My skittishness on Veeva had always been related to its valuation premium, which has since undergone a complete, clean reset. Amid strong billings and a decent guidance outlook for the current year, FY27, I'm raising my rating on Veeva to a Buy.

To me, the company's merits now far outweigh its potential risks. The core drivers of the upside case for Veeva are:

  • Veeva is unlikely to be displaced entirely by AI. Consider for a moment the fact that Veeva is a vertical software company that serves the biotech and pharmaceutical industries, which are highly regulated. Its software assists not just in "regular" tasks that other industries perform (such as sales/CRM), but its more specialized tools offer clinical operations support as well as regulatory submissions. It's highly unlikely that major life sciences giants like Moderna (MRNA) and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) will take a chance on ripping out mission-critical systems like Veeva in favor of new AI tools that don't have domain-specific knowledge.
  • Growth at scale. Despite being the juggernaut in the life sciences vertical software and hitting nearly $4 billion in annualized revenue, Veeva is still growing revenue and billings in the mid-teens, signaling plenty of room for expansion.
  • Stellar growth/profitability balance approaching the Rule of 60. Many software companies aspire merely to hit the "Rule of 40," which is calculated as revenue growth plus pro forma operating margins. Meanwhile, Veeva is guiding for a low-teens revenue growth on top of mid-40s margins in FY27, which positions the company just shy of a Rule of 60. In other words, the company's efficiency relative to its growth at scale is enviable in today's market and makes its waning valuation multiples more deserving of an upward reset.
  • Fortress balance sheet. The company has over $6.5 billion of cash on its balance sheet, debt-free. As Veeva's market cap declines, this cash position is becoming a substantial chunk of the company's current market value.

Valuation used to be Veeva's core weakness, but now, after its precipitous and sudden fall, it's a reason to buy the stock. At current share prices near $150, Veeva trades at a market cap of $24.70 billion. Netting off the substantial $6.56 billion of cash on Veeva's balance sheet yields an enterprise value of just $18.14 billion.

For FY27 (the year for Veeva ending in January 2027), Veeva is guiding to $3.59-$3.60 billion in revenue, which reflects 12-13% y/y growth, and $1.52 billion in operating cash flow (we note the company has very minor annual capex). It's guiding to $8.85 in pro forma EPS, which reflects 9% EPS growth.

This positions Veeva's multiples at:

  • 5.0x EV/FY27 revenue.
  • 11.4x EV/FY27 FCF.
  • 16.9x FY27 P/E, or 12.4x ex-cash P/E.

To me, the chance to buy this stock at a low-teens ex-cash P/E is quite compelling, especially given very little evidence that AI is anything but a tailwind for this company. Buy the dip here.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Veeva's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The fiscal Q4 (January quarter) results are shown below.

Veeva's revenue grew 16% y/y to $836.0 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $811.0 million (+12% y/y) by a wide four-point margin.

Now, there are both negatives and positives to watch out for in the quarter. One of the potential red flags here is that a large chunk of the revenue strength in Q4 was due to an uptick in services revenue, which grew 14% y/y (versus 9% growth in Q3) to $128.2 million.

Professional services revenue is performed at barely above cost, which makes the revenue mix shift dilutive to gross margins. Veeva's pro forma gross margins still remained high for a software company at 76.5%, and yet we do note this declined -60 bps y/y. That said, underlying subscription gross margins remained flat y/y and at high levels at 86.7%; as long as the company isn't facing a permanent mix shift toward lower-margin services work (which is essentially consulting/integration work done by Veeva staff), we shouldn't see sustained margin headwinds.

The bright side here is that Veeva showcased very strong billings. In Q4, billings of $1.51 billion far exceeded revenue (though that's typical seasonality for Veeva and for many software companies, which tend to close deals in the last quarter of the year as IT departments exhaust their budgets) and grew 18% y/y - accelerating five points sequentially versus 13% growth in Q3 and the best quarter for billings growth in FY26. This may be a potential leading indicator that Veeva's 12-13% revenue growth guide for FY27 may be on the conservative side.

For now, the company is also managing to sustain its margin expansion, with pro forma operating margins rising 110 bps y/y to 43.8%.

That said, we are also mindful of headcount growth. In FY26, the company added 637 net-new employees, growing headcount by 9% y/y. We also note that the company's guidance for FY27 implies a 44.2% pro forma operating margin, down -70 bps from 44.9% in FY26. We're hopeful that the company can begin to slow or cut headcount and leverage AI to drive higher efficiency in FY27 and beyond.

Risks And Key Takeaways

Of course, we should also be mindful of the risks that Veeva faces. While I don't believe that AI products can fully displace Veeva, I do think Veeva has a risk of seat-based contraction. Many of its products, such as its CRM tools, are based on the number of users. As companies continue to leverage AI to drive down headcount, Veeva could see a pullback in the number of paid seat subscriptions, slowing down its growth.

That said, it's difficult to say that this year's sharp >30% slide in Veeva's stock hasn't already appropriately priced in this risk. Buy the dip here with confidence.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: The Market Is Getting This Wrong Again (Upgrade)
data.description:
Anthropic Mythos Killing Cybersecurity? Are You Kidding Me?

Software stocks are getting absolutely clobbered into submission right now as I pen this update for Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW).

At this point, it doesn't seem to matter whether you are a cybersecurity stock, enterprise data software company, or infrastructure software or application software; the market has taken the hatchet to cut their exposure to the embattled industry.

And the selloff has already led to a further breakdown of oversold levels from February for SaaS (IGV), as sellers look to establish new lower support zones following the unveiling of Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) latest AI model.

To make matters worse for these software companies, Anthropic hasn’t even released it for general availability, because the AI company fears that its new Mythos AI model could wreak untold destruction on previously unknown or undetected "high-severity" vulnerabilities across the whole enterprise software ecosystem.

In times like this, I thought cybersecurity companies should have the upper hand in dealing with the fallout. However, this isn't the case. But, why?

Furthermore, for PANW, the company is also included as part of the early reviewers in Project Glasswing to preview the new AI model for them and (hopefully) address the vulnerabilities timely.

This is a measured and necessary step for PANW to work on the assessed gaps in their AI defense capabilities before Anthropic releases the model in the wild.

You should know that Palo Alto Networks is joined by CrowdStrike (CRWD) in this collaborative effort to address these incipient challenges that are clearly becoming more convoluted and difficult for even the cybersecurity gold standard to resolve.

Otherwise, why would they need to hold it back from general availability first, right?

It must have spooked the model developers enough that they think it's only the right thing to do so.

I do hope that we see more such collaborative efforts between AI companies and the cybersecurity partners, working in unison.

I also don't think there's any doubt that AI companies have proven their abilities to develop world-class foundation models that are now highly capable of exploiting cyber vulnerabilities. And this continues to evolve quickly.

Yet, real-time detection and response is deterministic and requires telemetry data and resources that are surely in the domain expertise of PALO and CRWD.

So, they must stand shoulder to shoulder as they work on significantly lowering the chances that the AI model could be used by threat actors in a way that the defenders haven't had a chance to fix.

Nevertheless, it now seems like my decision to call out PANW’s highly expensive valuation in my last update seems to be a sound move.

But, with the stock already appearing to have bottomed out over the $140 zone in February, is it time for us to turn bullish here?

I actually think so. PANW has been rated as a Buy in my Investing Group service for a while now (Ultimate Growth Investing).

And, I believe the age of AI agents will undoubtedly require more AI defense and not less, right?

Let’s get a few things right. Anthropic’s decision to bring Palo Alto Networks on board is a true testament to the cyber defender's world-class capabilities.

Among the 40 companies selected by Anthropic to work on this preview model, I believe it also augments Palo Alto’s platform approach to addressing and buttressing cybersecurity defenses.

Palo Alto Networks Leading The Platformization Drive

The platformization drive has already delivered solid growth back in the previous fiscal quarter.

With next-gen security ARR surpassing the $6.3 billion mark, the company guided to the $7.95 billion mark for fiscal Q3, implying a stellar >50% growth cadence. Still not fast enough?

It also plays nicely into the full-year cadence and outlook, suggesting that there are no indications of a slowdown that should worry investors for now.

If anything, I think the partnership with Anthropic shows us that while some cybersecurity companies without the platformization advantages of PANW could face significant disruption risks, that shouldn’t be the case for Palo Alto Networks.

A cursory glance at the various growth vectors within PANW’s business segments validates my conviction that the engine hasn’t sputtered.

While the stock obviously isn’t immune to sharp selloffs or spreading pessimism from software investors, I believe we need to have the gumption to differentiate top-notch cybersecurity companies that are highly resilient to mentioned disruption risks.

And in the case of PANW, I believe we could credibly point out that the ARR metrics across SASE (+40% YoY), software firewall (+25% YoY), and even XSIAM have been on fire.

I think it reinforces my point that as AI agents become even more threatening to governments and enterprises, organizations will look to spend more to beef up their AI defense and not less, won't they?

Also, it should be seen as a hybrid partnership where Anthropic works closely with the best cyber defenders in the business to help nullify these increasingly more powerful capabilities that could land in the hands of adversaries and unscrupulous threat actors.

Plus, overall spending metrics do seem to favor PANW and its cybersecurity peers, with IT spending budgets on this critical area potentially going up to 10%.

PANW: I Agree That High Valuation Is A Dampener

Nevertheless, I think the crux of the issue continues to revolve around whether the market believes whether PANW's relatively high-growth multiple has adequately captured the downside scenarios worrying investors right now.

Clearly, PANW is still rated highly for growth, with forward earnings multiples at >46x. And that’s based on adjusted earnings, right?

If we consider earnings quality as a criterion for those of you who prefer GAAP numbers, it’s almost 85x on forward earnings.

Yeah, I guess the problem with stock-based compensation is becoming a real bugbear for software investors.

And it's now like Palo Alto Networks has a low stock-based comp-to-revenue ratio, right? At 14%, it's actually in what I think is a quite high zone, although not as extreme as some of its smaller peers.

Nevertheless, while it’s less than CrowdStrike’s 23% metric, it’s far higher than Fortinet’s (FTNT), 4.1%, right?

Anyway, I think you get the gist. At this level of forward GAAP P/E, it’s fair to say that the stock isn’t priced for high execution risks at all, and a further selloff to compress the multiples also cannot be ruled out.

And the fact is that with the more advanced AI capabilities benefiting the adversaries, the potential for glaring mistakes, breaches, and hacks on Palo Alto’s customer base has also risen, right?

With threat actors now being able to capitalize on highly capable AI agents automating these hacks and breaches, it might become even harder for PANW to get it right all the time.

And for cybersecurity companies, the reputational risks from such mishaps could be disastrous if they suffer significant or multiple breaches, especially when their multiples don’t quite offer room for disappointment.

And for PANW, the gap between the forward adjusted P/E and the GAAP P/E offers another sore disconnect for investors to scrutinize, as they make the case that such monumental risks haven’t been fully captured into its current valuation.

Buy Or Sell PANW Stock?

Going into this week’s setup, I must say that PANW's print has stayed highly resilient, despite what appears to be another bout of panic selling for software stocks.

Given the elevated level of uncertainty right now, broad-based selling across SaaS names will inevitably affect even the most resolute of them, such as PANW.

But, I think as long as the $140 level can continue to be defended strongly by dip buyers, it shouldn't worsen the selloff and cause another round of panic selling to hit PANW in the process.

I do recognize that the high valuation risks will continue to act like a dampener against a rerating thesis, so to speak.

But I'm not going to let such fears creep into what I believe will be one of the key beneficiaries in the AI agentic era.

Top-notch cybersecurity providers such as Palo Alto Networks will be in the front running of securing enterprise and government workloads against global state actors keen on exploiting infrastructure hacks and significant vulnerabilities that were previously not believed to be possible.

The bullish case for PANW cannot be further overstated in this context.

Investors must be clear-eyed and separate the wheat from the chaff as they try to assess the disconnect happening in software stocks right now.

Rating: Upgrade PANW to Buy.

data.title: Safe Bulkers: Steady Execution, But The Easy Upside Looks Gone
data.description:
I last wrote about Safe Bulkers, Inc. (SB) in September. At the time, I described the company as a "calmer, cleaner ship" than the one I had previously covered in 2021. I argued that its modernized, compliance-ready dry bulk fleet and deep discount to NAV formed an attractive opportunity. Since then, SB stock has surged 43% and has almost doubled over the past 12 months.

The rally makes sense, as the dry-bulk market has improved at a similar pace. Shipping looks quite attractive today, in general, as vessels are becoming increasingly critical for supply chains (containerships), and food and energy security (dry bulk and tankers). Indeed, the Baltic Dry Index, in particular, has surged, and investors have chased almost anything with leverage to freight. Safe Bulkers has been along for the ride, and while I still view it as one of the best-managed companies in the space, I don't think future gains will be as easy, and so I maintain a neutral view.

The Story Is As Solid As It Gets

Safe Bulkers hasn't stopped modernizing its fleet, which, together with the surge in rates, has helped the company capitalize on the dry bulk rally. The company ended Q4 with a 45-vessel fleet of 4.6 million deadweight tons. The average age of its vessels was a relatively young 10.5 years.

And considering that 12 of its vessels were built from 2022 onward and met IMO GHG Phase 3 and NOx Tier III standards, which matter to charterers, I am not surprised that the company has the means to capture the dry bulk rally quite easily.

Today, the company also has eight Kamsarmax newbuilds on order, including two methanol dual-fuel ships. Four are scheduled for delivery this year, with the remainder arriving slowly through 2029. As long as Safe Bulkers maintains a young, fuel-flexible fleet, it should have both a commercial advantage and a greater margin of safety than other players with older fleets.

At the same time, I think the company has been smart regarding selling older vessels. For example, they sold the 2012-built Michalis H in February for $35.2 million, a price level that no one would have expected three years ago.

The Chartering Strategy Works

Beyond its young fleet, I also remain a fan of Safe Bulkers' chartering strategy, which should work well even under weak environments, let alone today's much stronger one. The company still maintains a healthy mix of period contracts and spot exposure, which provides some protection to cash flow without closing off the upside if the market strengthens further.

Indeed, TCEs continued to rise throughout last year's rally, as spot exposure remained sufficient. This trend should have endured YTD, as by the time the company reported Q4 results, it had 30 vessels on period charters and 17 in the spot market. Also, roughly 42% of the 2026 fleet days were already covered, with contracted revenue of about $177.6 million before any scrubber-related benefit.

The Capesize side seems strong too. Seven vessels were on period charters at an average daily rate of $24,206, with an average remaining duration of about 1.8 years. That is a fairly sensible "hedging" between capturing the rally for extra upside and securing sufficient cash flow to cover the dividend and keep recycling the fleet in a predictable manner.

So, the way rates remain on the rise, Safe Bulkers should continue to see rising TCEs into Q1 and potentially Q2. Remember that China's iron-ore imports in the first two months of 2026 climbed 10% YoY to 210.02 million metric tons, on top of iron-ore imports hitting a record 1.26 billion tons last year. That's a resilient trend forming.

I don't see why this tailwind won't endure, especially given the possibility of longer-haul cargoes from Guinea’s Simandou project and the expected supply-side friction from emissions rules and slower steaming.

The Valuation Might Signal A Ceiling

As I just discussed, Safe Bulkers continues to present a very compelling case given the mix of favorable market conditions and fleet management. Where I am hitting an obstacle with adopting a bullish stance on the stock, though, is the valuation.

Following the stock doubling over the past year, it is trading at around 0.8x its GAAP book value. That might sound cheap, but in shipping, it often is not, and much wider discounts tend to be the norm. As you can see in the chart below, historically, Safe Bulkers has topped at a P/B between 0.8 and 1.1x.

Of course, the company's GAAP book value isn't too accurate most of the time, especially following the recent increase in rates, which naturally boosts its fleet value. But even then, the difference isn't as big as you would think.

Even if you assume somewhat fair to modestly high prices of about $53 million for a 10-year-old Capesize, $30 million for a 10-year-old Kamsarmax, and maybe $21 million for a 15-year-old Panamax, and adjust it for the rest of the fleet, Safe Bulker's NAV likely comes closer to $10, in my view. But I would argue that GAAP book value is likely a better indicator and reflects a more "average" situation, as today's valuations could just as easily fall. And that's coming from someone who believes that dry bulk rates could continue to rise. It's just that in shipping, one should always require a wider margin of safety, in my view.

And frankly, the fact that the dividend yield has been compressed significantly following this past year's rally due to the lack of dividend hikes means that the stock's total return prospects have become increasingly speculative.

The stock now offers a five-year low yield of 3%, which I find lacking. During the upcycle, I expect to receive hefty dividends from a shipping stock to reflect the risk of holding these very cyclical securities. Don't get me wrong, I do like Safe Bulker's prudent capital management, but that doesn't mean I will accept such a low yield while buying the stock at a potentially peak-cycle valuation.

Final Thoughts

I maintain my Safe Bulkers rating to a Hold at today's prices. I do believe that Safe Bulkers remains one of the better-run names in the space. The fleet is young, and capital management remains steady and prudent. But after a sharp re-rating, making money on the stock might not be as easy as a few months ago. Besides the lack of a deeper discount, the yield itself is also too soft for my taste, considering this is shipping we're talking about.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Profit-Taking In Stocks Ahead Of Key Weekend Risk
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The ceasefire still stands, but markets are seeking further confirmation as US-Iran talks are set to proceed this weekend.

A significant barrier to the negotiations had been the ongoing Israeli attacks against Hezbollah in Lebanon, with the Islamic regime trying to hold onto its remaining strongholds in the Crescent.

Israel-Lebanon direct talks will begin on Tuesday, and strikes have been limited in the past 24 hours after a heated exchange between Trump and Netanyahu. Hezbollah continued waves of rocket and drone attacks since.

Still applying heavy leverage on the Strait of Hormuz ahead of the talks, only 4 tankers have crossed today, showing only minimal progress despite the ongoing truce.

While Iran has been pushing back on the discussions, creating internal tensions over who will be appointed to the Islamabad (Pakistan) talks, recent headlines have confirmed that it will participate.

Good news? Surely. This is better than nothing, particularly with the US side seemingly ready to make concessions to end the war sooner rather than later (after ~6 weeks of war).

But looking at the stock market today, traders have already priced them in and will now expect to see decisive progress in the coming days.

As per usual, the main point will be to see what's next for the Strait of Hormuz and, more particularly, crude oil prices, still stuck around the $98 to $100 tight range.

Any prediction of what is going to happen is nothing short of a gamble – the Trump Administration is serious about the talks, with midterms approaching and this war not faring well with the American population.

Demands will be strict, and the Iranian delegation will be under pressure.

It would be unrealistic to see an actual deal at the end of the weekend, but markets will be leaning on White House communications.

Odds for a proper peace deal before April 30 are still only priced below 20%. Traders will want to see a major gap down in crude oil prices to push stock prices higher.

Nasdaq was the only index to rally today, but sentiment is progressively souring as participants are now increasingly taking profits on a very positive week, and all benchmarks are now turning red.

As traders are getting ready for the final trading hours of the week, we will look at the short-term intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The profit-taking has been broad across the entire stock market, with participants easing their weekly purchases and rotating back into the names less affected by wartime flows (including semiconductors in Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO)).

Dow Jones 2H Chart and Trading Levels

Dow Jones is now rejecting the higher bound of its upward channel and imminently breaking the key 48,000 level.

This could lead to profit-taking acceleration toward the afternoon, but such trading levels have low odds of holding after the key weekend negotiation period.

  • Good news would easily see a gap higher above 48,300
  • A negative scenario would look to test 47,000
    • Any break below puts wartime trading back into action
  • Further uncertainty hints at progressive downside back to the 47,400 - 47,600 support

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • March 4 resistance 48,250 - 48,300 (bull above)
  • Mini-resistance 48,700
  • Major Resistance – 49,000 to 49,200

Support Levels

  • Bull/Bear Momentum Pivot at 48,000 (+/- 100 points)
  • Major Pivotal Support 47,400 to 47,600 (50-Hour MA)
  • War Resistance now Key Support 47,000 +/- 100 Points (Bearish below)
  • March 8 War lows Resistance now Support 46,300
  • 45,700 to 45,900 August Support
  • January 2025 Highs 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 2H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq has just reached the top of its 25,250 resistance zone, and momentum is now largely cooling, pointing to some downside ahead.

With the weekend action still uncertain, the end-afternoon session should still see continued profit-taking back towards the 24,900 Intraday Momentum Zone.

Breaching it on Monday could see a quick test of 24,500 and below if the tone sours.

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Key Resistance 25,000 to 25,250 (top at 25,250!)
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb Range resistance
  • Major resistance 25,700 to 25,850

Support Levels

  • 24,750 to 24,900 Momentum Pivot
  • 24,450 to 24,550 Pivotal Support
  • Feb Range Support 24,150 to 24,200
  • Major 2026 Pivotal Support 23,800 to 24,000
  • August 2025 Support 23,500 to 23,650
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 2H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is forming a similar turn in RSI momentum as Nasdaq, hinting at some downside towards the afternoon.

The action may stall at the 6,760 to 6,780 Pivot Zone in the waiting for further clarity.

  • Downside continuation will point to 6,700.
  • Good news should break 6,840 and may even extend to 6,900.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Early March Resistance 6,820 to 6,840 (rejecting)
  • Key Resistance Zone 6,880 to 6,900
  • Previous ATH Resistance 6,945 to 6,975

Support Levels

  • Major Momentum Pivot 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,680 to 6,700 Pivotal Support (4H 200-period MA)
  • 6,580 to 6,610 Support
  • 4H 50-period MA 6,550
  • 6,490 to 6,520 October lows
  • 6,300 psychological level (War lows)

The narrative is easing, but keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the weekend to stay ahead of the game.

data.title: Tesla: Car Company Aspiring High-Tech, But Stock Performance Confirms Neither
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has lost 21% of its value since my last report, aligning well with my "Sell" rating. What continuously holds for Tesla is that the company is an automotive company with battery exposure but presents itself as a tech or tech-aspiring company that should largely justify its premium trading levels. However, that also means that when uncertainty increases in the marketplace, the premium comes under pressure rather quickly. In this report, I take a look at the company’s stock price performance, the expectations for Q1, and whether Tesla shares now provide a more compelling investment opportunity.

Tesla Share Prices Show It Underperformed

If we put Tesla’s share price development together with several industry-specific ETFs, we see that it trades neither as a car company nor as the tech company it wants to be. Tesla share prices increased 27% over the past 12 months. The First Trust S-Network Future Vehicles & Tech ETF (CARZ) and the Smart Mobility ETF (HAIL) gained 76% and 44% over the same period. The technology-oriented ETFs State Street Technology Select Sector (XLK) and iShares Future AI & Tech ETF (ARTY) returned 40% and 62%, respectively. So, Tesla is the underperformer of the pack, and it does not quite vibe with the perception many investors have that Tesla is the best investment out there. Over a three-year period, Tesla did outperform the Smart Mobility ETF and the Future AI Tech ETF (ARTY) while slightly outperforming the CARZ ETF, but underperforming XLK. So, the reality is that the combination of automotive and tech and the way Tesla presents itself, even with an extremely high EV/EBITDA ratio, has not driven strong outperformance. As an investment, Tesla has been mediocre against the risk and valuation in my view.

Expectations For Tesla Have Been Lowered

Tesla’s stock drifted lower as the company reported Q1 deliveries that failed to meet already soft expectations for Q1. The company is facing tariff pressures and increasing competition from China in a rather soft macro-outlook. So, soft deliveries are not completely unexpected, but for a company that trades at the levels of Tesla, it leads to a rather large decompression of the EV/EBITDA trading multiple. For Q1, analysts are expecting 17.6% growth in revenues and 40.3% growth in sales, which don’t seem like bad numbers at all. However, over the last three months, 32 revisions on revenues have been down and four revisions up, and earnings revisions showed 30 downward revisions and nine revisions upward. So, the revision trend is negative but not dramatic in the sense that the three-month trend shows a 1.5% decline in revenue estimates and 8% in earnings, which is more substantial.

For this year, revenue estimates have come down by 6% compared to six months ago and by 8.1% and 11.4% for 2027 and 2028. Earnings per share estimates have been lowered by 9% this year to $2.03 and by 16.4% and 20.8%, respectively, for the years after, bringing the estimates to $2.73 and $4.06. So, there is a meaningful reduction in the expectations not just this year but for the years ahead as well.

Tesla Is A 'Hold' With Caution

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Tesla is not my favorite stock. The stock price is high, with valuation levels being stretched on expectations that Tesla will transform itself from an EV company to a frontrunner in autonomy and robotics. The apparent advantage the company has is that it knows how to collect and process staggering amounts of data. That should help them in becoming a successful autonomy and robotics company. However, more often than not, the projections made by Elon Musk on commercialization paths have not been met. So, there is risk as well. I have upgraded Tesla’s stock from "Sell" to "Hold" and that is on a 3-year median EV/EBITDA base, which is lower than the longer-term base, and on the condition that Tesla grows itself into the company that Elon Musk envisions. Until that time, it is a speculative opportunity. I am currently assigning a "Hold" rating with a $449.52 price target, noting the various risks that Tesla’s transformation efforts bring in terms of execution risk and automotive pressures in the form of macro pressures and competition.

The margins are currently expected to dip this year, with improvements next year as growth revitalizes. The company is in a net cash position, so the increase in capital expenditures that will lead to negative cash flow this year, just like last year, is not immediately concerning, but we note that the surge in capital expenditures puts exponentially more pressure on Tesla to get commercialization timelines right, and, as noted, the execution record of Tesla is not great in that regard.

Conclusion: Tesla Is High-Risk With Acceptable Reward

Tesla remains valued extremely high, and we see that the company’s share price has not performed well against car markers or the tech industry. So, the high-tech company that Tesla aspires to be and its current reality of being a car company with a high focus on autonomy have not delivered worthwhile returns for shareholders yet. That may change, and in fact it has to change, because if it doesn't, then Tesla’s premium valuation will not or should not hold. I am upgrading the stock from "Sell" to "Hold" as we note that downside based on 2027 earnings is now less than 10%, and upside against the 3-year median EV/EBITDA does imply upside. Execution is where things are going to matter, and that is where Elon Musk needs to sharpen his focus.

data.title: HF Foods: I Don't Buy Wall Street's Bull Case
data.description:
Tariffs, weak traffic at Asian restaurants, and now...an overhang!?

FY 2025 was lukewarm for HF Foods Group Inc. (HFFG), and FY 2026 doesn’t smell any better.

Well, the stock is already down almost 80% since its IPO in 2018 and just over 50% in the last year. While I wasn't as bullish as Wall Street (after all, they've held the 'Strong Buy' recommendation for a while), I stayed somewhere in the middle with a 'Hold.'

Can't say that was my best call.

Since my last article here last November, the stock has fallen just over 4%. It lost to the S&P once again. And, to be honest, I adopted a much more bearish tone in the last article.

HF Foods was the classic 'cheap for a reason.'

But it looked too cheap to dump, and if you were in around $2, it was better to wait for some kind of catalyst.

What could that be? That might be your question.

If you ask me, I'd imagine them taking advantage of the softness of the economy, which is probably leaving a lot of distributors bankrupt, and scooping up some of their local competitors.

That fits the thesis pretty well.

HF Foods, the main supplier to small ethnic restaurants, could be set up for a rebound if traffic comes back with more experimental concepts.

The CEO himself told us that their TAM is ~$50 billion, and with a revenue of $1.2 billion, they would be sitting at a market share of ~2 to 3%, being the 'big guys.'

That is, a lot of room to grow. And the IPO was already a first attempt at that.

But there are a few bumps here.

  1. Family-run Asian restaurants (HF Foods’ core customers) are struggling in a value-driven market dominated by big chains. Without scale, they can't keep up, and they end up buying less.
  2. The Debt/EBITDA ratio is above 4.5x (also considering ST debt), making debt-driven M&A out of reach. Issuing debt would simply be too expensive.
  3. With $100 million in ATM deals on the table, they could dilute the shareholder stake by almost 50% to raise capital for the M&A. That's not good at all, especially if they don't get a good ROI here.

And, I think to top off the bad omen, management kind of said that FY 2026 would be similar to FY 2025.

Based on current trends, we expect 2026 to be like 2025 with low single-digit growth on the top line as well as the bottom line for both adjusted EBITDA and gross profit.

To start, how about I give my two cents on dilution?

Dilution Will Be No Small Matter

Just like with PE overhangs, I don't think any investor likes to lay their head on the pillow worrying about an ATM overhang.

If this sounds unfamiliar, it simply means issuing new shares and selling them on the open market.

About $100 million, to be more precise.

Well, if they sell them at $2.10 per share (where the stock is trading today), that's ~47.6 million shares issued (just over 100 million shares outstanding). A brutal dilution of almost 50% for the shareholder.

That's only if they fully utilize the ATM and the price stays flat. It won't happen like that, but it gives you a ballpark.

It all depends on how accretive these acquisitions they're eyeing will be.

For a leveraged small-cap like HF Foods, I imagine the ROI of this acquisition would need to be somewhere between ~10 and 15% to overcome the cost of capital (~$10 to $15 million in incremental EBITDA over time, just to simplify things a bit here).

Is this even possible in this economy?

Before I try to answer that, let me imagine some scenarios for these acquisitions.

$100 million is a huge check. There's no denying that, especially in a fragmented market like specialty food distribution.

So, my biggest guess is that they will focus on tuck-ins from small or medium-sized distributors. They are probably choosing them to increase their client portfolio or warehouses in grey areas (such as in the North or Northeast of the country, for example).

It's something similar to what they did a few years ago with Great Wall Seafood.

If you use that deal as a baseline, HF Foods bought it for $44 million (~0.25x sales). Assuming they were also targeting a multiple of ~6x EBITDA, the incremental EBITDA here would be around just over $7 million.

With the $100 million in hand and pinpointing a slightly more normalized multiple (the Great Wall deal was during the pandemic), HF Foods could scoop up a company worth ~$250 million based on 0.4x sales.

Typical of fragmented markets, don't you think? It's a consolidator moat.

My guess is that they will do multiple transactions and probably won't use all the capacity unless it's a very strong pipeline.

Some three or four deals, or two like Great Wall.

Returning to the question I left open, would they be able to generate that 10-15% return to compensate for that dilution?

That's a difficult question to answer.

Looking at companies of that type, it's very difficult for them to achieve that return unless they really achieve significant efficiencies.

Just take a look at the ROTC of other distributors out there:

HF Foods is back to being a roll-up story, just like at the IPO. From here, it all comes down to dilution and what kind of return they can get on it.

Will FY 2026 Be Like FY 2025?

Management is calling for FY 2026 to look like FY 2025. Does that rule out acquisitions this year? I really don't know how to answer that.

Let me know what you think in the comments; it's always good to hear your take.

If they are talking about organic growth, revenue would grow in the low single digits to $1.25 billion. Remember that I'm not considering the ramp-up of the Atlanta plant here since management is projecting it for H2 FY 2026 or FY 2027.

I also expect SG&A to decrease slightly relative to revenue (consolidation of call centers and new ERP full rollout). To counterbalance this, I also imagine the gross margin will remain tight at ~17%.

EBITDA margins should keep bouncing around that 2.5 to 3% range.

OCF is more complicated.

HF Foods is indeed generating positive OCF and even some chunk in FCF, but a large part of that is them stretching Accounts Receivable (~$24 million in FY 2025 alone). If you remove that, FCF would be red.

Not to mention post-acquisition write-downs. You can probably guess that this isn't high-quality cash.

My question is whether the ATM goes toward acquisitions or cleaning up the balance sheet.

Anyway, it still seems to me that Wall St. is wrong here. Not regarding revenue (their projection is $1.27 billion for FY 2026, about 4% higher year-over-year), but regarding EPS.

In what world would they go from a normalized EPS of -$0.07 to $0.35 in FY 2026 and then almost double that to $0.60 in FY 2027?

Way too far, I think.

That would be a net income of $18.5 million without considering potential dilution. There was no room for that last year, and I doubt there will be in FY 2027 either.

To me, if they're really going for a turnaround, it's going to be a slower one. Pick up a few companies this year and next, and maybe margins will normalize around FY 2028 or FY 2029.

And I'm not even talking about the macro picture.

Building Some Scenarios

To get straight to the point, I'll select a few scenarios for my modeling:

  • Without considering dilution, my base scenario projects revenue of $1.25 billion and EBITDA between $31.2 million and $37.5 million. There are 53 million shares outstanding.
  • Scoop up two medium-sized businesses at approximately 8x EBITDA and spend around $60 million; the incremental EBITDA would be $7.5 million (total of $41.8 million). The issuance would be at least 25 million (outstanding shares of approximately 78 to 80 million).
  • Using all $100 million for M&A, EBITDA could increase to almost $47 million. Outstanding shares could increase to approximately 100 million.
  • Scenario in which EBITDA rises to the $50 to $55 million range, with shares climbing to 100 million.

Unlike last time, I'm going to adjust the peers to some specialty distributors.

Here I'll use Calavo Growers (CVGW), Mission Produce (AVO), Innovative (IVFH), G. Willi-Foods (WILC), and AMCON (DIT). This would give us a multiple of ~9.7x.

In the first scenario (nothing happens), this EBITDA range gives me a price target of ~$2.25 for the stock. Note that this is very close to my $2.40 from the last article.

With two mid-sized M&A deals, you're looking at something like a $2.50 price target.

This wouldn't change much for a $100 million M&A scenario (also close to ~$2.50 per share), but if they achieve $50 to $55 million in EBITDA through synergies, the price target would be between $2.70 and $3.20.

Pretty clear, without those synergies, dilution is basically a wash.

Here's a peek at how this works in a chart for you:

With all this on the table, HF Foods is still a 'Hold' for me.

As you can see, the downside is small, and the upside isn't that big to add now.

The upside here hinges on the ROI they get from these acquisitions and if it can offset dilution. If you're already a holder, I think it's worth waiting to try and lock in this double-digit upside.

I would lean towards a 'Sell' here if they get through at least two more quarters without significant M&A in mind and with low-quality OCF.

I think EBITDA won't decline (the margin should remain stable thanks to the ERP rollout and SG&A improvements, as I've already mentioned), and the stock will keep hovering where it is until the acquisition catalyst.
